items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: Who developed iconological method in Studies in Iconology 1939?
items.why: Correct answer: Erwin Panofsky. “Heinrich Wolfflin” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1998 Art and Agency treats artworks as social agents?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gell. “Bourdieu” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many screens does the HL Comparative Study require maximum?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 screens. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
